content.full_text:
1355 42nd Street
Sacramento, CA 95819
November 14, 1990


Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Sir/Madam:

I would appreciate a written opinion regarding the definition
of the term "proceeding" as it applies to the different
administrative actions that occur in connection with the issuance
of private activity bonds by California Finance Authorities.
Between 1983 and 1989 I served as an appointee of the State
Treasurer in the position of Executive Secretary of the
California Pollution Control Financing Authority. I then worked
as a private consultant helping companies shape financial
strategies involving different aspects of bond issuance. I
did not function as a lobbyist or otherwise serve as an
intermediary between companies and state agencies.

I was among many former state government officials who entered
the business of bond issuance in one capacity or another during
the 1980s. This trend surely will continue during the next
decade. I know most of these individuals believed that it
was permissible for them to work with companies which previously
participated in an activity involving their former agencies
long as at least six months had elapsed since they functioned
in any decision-making role. Further, they believed there
were no restrictions on working with these companies if the
effort involved a particular proceeding distinct from the
one in which they participated as a decision-maker.

Before accepting compensation from any company, I called the
FPPC to inquire about guidelines and how they apply to the
work I envisioned. I identified myself and described in detail
the different proceedings connected with bond issuance. Based
on those discussions with your staff, it has been my
understanding that the issuance of private activity bonds
(as well as certain other types) involves several distinct
proceedings which may occur over a period of time that could
run for years. The following are some principal proceedings
we identified.

One proceeding revolves around the public hearing on a company's
request for an Initial Resolution. When this occurs, the
staff must review a company's application, summarize it for
the members of the Authority, and schedule it as an agenda
item in a properly noticed public hearing. The Initial
Resolution is numbered separately from a subsequent Final
Resolution, considered in a separate public hearing, with
a separate staff recommendation. It can only be approved
pursuant to a roll call vote in which it is supported by a
majority of the Authority members. The proceeding is acknowledged
with separate legal certification to the company and it carries
a legally distinct meaning under federal law. When a majority
of the members of the Authority vote to approve the Initial
Resolution, the proceeding is completed.

The public hearing on a Final Resolution constitutes a separate
proceeding involving a different review process, different
policy guidelines, different outside participants, independent
feasibility or environmental studies, and distinct legal
requirements, including acceptance by the Attorney General.
Hearings on Final Resolutions are treated as separate proceedings
under the Administrative Procedures Act and are conducted
as a separate part the public meeting agenda by financing
authorities. They are not considered mere continuations
or extensions of the Initial Resolution hearing. Final
Resolutions are designated using a distinct numerical sequence.
Testimony is presented by government officials, company
representatives, support and opposition. The proceeding is
completed with a roll call vote on the Final Resolution.

The request for bond allocation from the California Debt Limit
Allocation Committee involves another separate proceeding.
The staff of this agency conduct a review and make
recommendations based on separate policies which change each
year. The proceeding is completed with a roll call vote which
determines whether allocation is awarded.

A request for a "refinancing" or "reissuance" or renewal of
an "escrow" bond issue also triggers a separate proceeding.
It requires notice of a new public hearing, the opportunity
to present new oral and written testimony, appointment by
the State Treasurer of key outside participants, preparation
of new bond documents, a distinct financing justification,
and separate acceptance by the California Attorney General.
With so-called "escrow" bonds" redemption is automatic unless
the Authority takes separate action at a separate proceeding
which concludes with a roll call vote regarding new bonds.

Sale of bonds by the State Treasurer is also a distinct
proceeding with different requirements, legal determinations
and bond documents. The proceeding involves many internal
checks and decisions by administrative officials. It concludes
with the official bond closing.

These assumptions regarding the separate and distinct nature
of the different proceedings, which I arrived at following
several telephone conversations with your office, have guided
my consulting activities and those of many others engaged
in the private business aspects of bond issuance by California
financing authorities. If I have misunderstood your
interpretation of the term "proceeding" as it applies to these
matters, I ask that you clarify your position as quickly as
possible. If we have a difference of opinion regarding the
definition of the term "proceeding" in bond issuing activities,
it is vitally important for all involved to clearly understand
your interpretation in order to avoid misunderstandings now
and in the future.


Sincerely,

[signature]

Douglas E. Chandler

sections.question: What is the definition of the term 'proceeding' as it applies to the different administrative actions that occur in connection with the issuance of private activity bonds by California Financing Authorities?
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks for a written FPPC opinion on whether different stages of private activity bond issuance (Initial Resolution hearing, Final Resolution hearing, bond allocation request, refinancing, and bond sale) constitute separate and distinct proceedings under FPPC conflict-of-interest rules, or if they are extensions of a single proceeding.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section exists. The document details the requestor's understanding of separate proceedings based on prior telephone discussions with FPPC staff, but seeks formal written clarification. Some OCR errors present (e.g., 'woufld', 'cAfifornia', 'wj-th').

embedding.summary: Douglas Chandler, a former Executive Secretary of the California Pollution Control Financing Authority (1983-1989), requests FPPC clarification on whether various administrative actions in private activity bond issuance constitute separate proceedings, as this definition affects restrictions on his consulting work with companies previously involved in financing authority activities.
